home.tagline: Join us for our Madagascar celebration
home.joinUs: Do you want to join us?
home.introText: As you might know, we have friends and family in different parts of the world. Therefore, next to our intimate celebration in Belgium, we will also celebrate in Imerintsiatosika, Madagascar on 14 November this year (only one to two hours away from the capital, Antananarivo).
home.rsvpText: Are you interested in flying to Madagascar in November to celebrate with us? We would love to see you there. Please RSVP as soon as possible so we can count you in and keep you updated.
home.tripInfoIntro: On this page you can access some extra information to help you prepare for your trip if you are a first time traveler to Madagascar.
home.accommodationText: Book yourself an accommodation in Antananarivo for the duration of the wedding (13–15th November). We can arrange the transfer to the wedding location for you. There are multiple options, depending on your budget and comfort needs.
home.accommodationTransfer: Transfer to the wedding location in Imerintsiatosika will be arranged
home.accommodationOptions: Multiple accommodation options available at various price points
home.travelText: Do you want to travel before or after the wedding? We know a local tour operator that can organize a tour with you and the other guests, depending on your travel wishes and length of stay.
home.deadlineText: Please let us know by 15 March if you would like to make use of our contact. Tours and accommodation for November are booked out quickly.
home.tourOperator: A trusted local tour operator is available to help with planning
home.tourWithGuests: Possibility to join organized tours with other wedding guests
home.bookQuickly: Tours and accommodation book out quickly — plan ahead!
home.newsletterSubtitle: Subscribe to receive updates about our Madagascar celebration
home.subscribeSuccess: Thank you! You'll receive updates soon.
home.subscribeError: Please enter a valid email address.
home.questionsText: Feel free to reach out to us anytime

planYourTrip.paragraph1: Madagascar is a beautiful country, so you might want to consider doing a tour before or after the wedding date. Antananarivo, the capital, will be a great starting point.
planYourTrip.paragraph2: For your safety, we advise against travelling all by yourself. Booking a tour with a trustworthy tour operator and an experienced guide is the safest option.
planYourTrip.paragraph3: Some accommodations book out quickly for November, so don't wait too long. We're happy to help you get started.
planYourTrip.inspirationText: Need some first inspiration? Here some amazing travel tips:
planYourTrip.card1Bullet1: Antananarivo, the capital, is the ideal starting point
planYourTrip.card1Bullet2: Plan a tour before or after the wedding on November 14
planYourTrip.card2Bullet1: Book with a trustworthy local tour operator
planYourTrip.card2Bullet2: An experienced guide helps with the language barrier
planYourTrip.card2Bullet3: Enjoy a stress-free and unforgettable experience
planYourTrip.card3DeadlineText: Let us know before 15 March if you'd like us to connect you with a local tour operator.
planYourTrip.regionNorthDescription: Where turquoise lagoons meet ancient forests. Drift between the islands of the Nosy Be archipelago — where whale sharks glide through warm waters — then head inland to the otherworldly red spires of Tsingy Rouge and the orchid-filled trails of Montagne d'Ambre. This is Madagascar at its most tropical and cinematic.
planYourTrip.regionWestDescription: A landscape that feels borrowed from another planet. Walk among 800-year-old baobab trees as the sunset turns the Avenue of the Baobabs into pure gold, then venture into the razor-sharp limestone cathedral of Tsingy de Bemaraha, a UNESCO World Heritage site. Nearby Kirindy hides the world's smallest primate, the Pygmy Mouse Lemur.
planYourTrip.regionCenterDescription: The soul of Madagascar lives here. Journey to the sacred UNESCO hill of Ambohimanga, the spiritual heart of the Merina kingdom, while rolling green hills stitched with terraced rice paddies stretch in every direction. It is in this timeless landscape, at Imerintsiatosika, that we will celebrate our wedding.
planYourTrip.regionEastDescription: Wake to the haunting call of the Indri — the world's largest living lemur — echoing through misty rainforest canopy in Andasibe, Madagascar's most rewarding wildlife park. Then glide along the Pangalanes Canal past palm-fringed fishing villages before reaching the vanilla coast, which supplies over half the world's vanilla.
planYourTrip.regionSouthDescription: Follow the legendary Route Nationale 7 south until Madagascar's 'Grand Canyon' appears: Isalo National Park, with its soaring sandstone canyons, hidden waterfalls, and jewel-toned natural pools. End your journey where the spiny forest meets the sea at Ifaty — a surreal, cactus-studded coast giving way to warm, laid-back beaches.
planYourTrip.mapClickHint: Click on a region for more information
planYourTrip.mapAlt: Illustrated map of Madagascar showing North, East, West, and South regions

essentialTravelTips.whatToExpectIntro: Madagascar is unlike anywhere else — a vast island with extraordinary wildlife, a rich mix of African, Austronesian and Arab cultures, and a beautifully relaxed pace of life. Travelling here is an incredible experience, but it helps to know a few things before you go. Here are our top practical tips so you can travel with confidence.
essentialTravelTips.climateBullet1: November marks the start of the rainy season — expect hot, humid days with temperatures around 25–32 °C.
essentialTravelTips.climateBullet2: Short but intense tropical downpours are common. Pack a lightweight rain jacket or poncho.
essentialTravelTips.climateBullet3: Evenings can be surprisingly cool, especially in the highlands around Antananarivo. Bring a light layer.
essentialTravelTips.paymentBullet1: Madagascar runs on cash. Bring euros in cash — you'll need them for the visa on arrival and as a backup.
essentialTravelTips.paymentBullet2: Check the EUR → Ariary exchange rate before you fly. You can exchange money at the airport or in banks.
essentialTravelTips.paymentBullet3: Card payments work in some city hotels and restaurants, but don't rely on them. Mastercard acceptance is patchy — a Visa card (or Revolut) is your safest bet.
essentialTravelTips.paymentBullet4: Withdraw cash at ATMs in Antananarivo before heading out of the city. Visa cards work at most machines; Mastercard is hit-or-miss.
essentialTravelTips.povertyBullet1: Madagascar is one of the world's poorest countries. You will see people — including children — asking for money. This can be confronting.
essentialTravelTips.povertyBullet2: As hard as it may feel, avoid giving money or food on the street. It can attract unwanted attention and reinforces a cycle that keeps children out of school.
essentialTravelTips.povertyBullet3: If you'd like to help, consider donating to a reputable local organisation — we can suggest a few.
essentialTravelTips.safetyBullet1: Madagascar is generally safe for tourists, but opportunistic pickpocketing does happen — especially in Antananarivo's busy markets.
essentialTravelTips.safetyBullet2: Stick to well-known areas, travel with a guide, and avoid walking around after dark.
essentialTravelTips.safetyBullet3: Use hotel-arranged transfers or your tour operator's transport rather than hailing rides on the street.
essentialTravelTips.safetyBullet4: Leave flashy jewellery at home and keep phones and cameras out of sight when not in use.
essentialTravelTips.infrastructureBullet1: Roads outside the capital can be rough — unpaved, potholed, and worse after rain. Long drives take longer than you'd expect.
essentialTravelTips.infrastructureBullet2: Motion sickness is common on winding roads. Bring medication if you're prone to it.
essentialTravelTips.infrastructureBullet3: Domestic flights exist but delays are the norm rather than the exception.
essentialTravelTips.infrastructureBullet4: Build a buffer day or two into your itinerary. Flexibility is the best travel hack here.
essentialTravelTips.healthBullet1: November means mosquitoes. Malaria risk is lower in the highlands but present elsewhere. Talk to your doctor about antimalarial medication and pack a strong DEET-based repellent.
essentialTravelTips.healthBullet2: Traveller's diarrhoea is common. Follow the golden rule: cook it, peel it, or skip it. Drink only bottled or boiled water, and avoid ice, unpeeled fruit, and undercooked food.
essentialTravelTips.healthBullet3: Pro tip: start taking probiotics a week before your trip, and pack a small kit with Imodium, rehydration salts, and activated charcoal.
essentialTravelTips.healthBullet4: Get travel insurance that covers medical evacuation — it's non-negotiable for a destination like Madagascar.
essentialTravelTips.healthBullet5: As tempting as they are, don't touch or feed animals — even friendly-looking ones can carry disease.
essentialTravelTips.informSubtitle: Come prepared and avoid the unexpected!
essentialTravelTips.informDescription: Before booking your trip, please inform yourself about the current situation in the country. The travel and safety directions of the foreign office can give you a good insight regarding risks and safety measures, as well as needed preparations before entering the country.
essentialTravelTips.preparationsIntro: You are wondering what you need to prepare for besides booking your travels? Here are some points you should consider before starting your trip.
essentialTravelTips.passportDescription: Do you still have a passport that is valid for 6 months after entering the country? If not, now is a great time to renew your passport!
essentialTravelTips.visaDescription: You can get a visa on arrival. Remember to withdraw some cash before the flight to pay for the visa — card payment might not always work, but you can directly pay in EUR.
essentialTravelTips.vaccinationsDescription: Though in most cases, there is no compulsory vaccination needed when travelling from Europe, it is worthwhile to inform yourself at a tropical institute or your GP what type of vaccinations are recommended for your travel plans. Better safe than sorry!
essentialTravelTips.healthPrepDescription: Prepare for mosquitoes: pack strong insect repellent and talk to your doctor to get malaria pills. Prepare a bag with travel medications including pills against motion sickness, charcoal pills, and Imodium.
